first_paragraph: [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") is the [largest religion](/wiki/Religion_in_Nepal "Religion in Nepal") in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 2006, the country declared itself a [secular country](/wiki/Secular_country "Secular country") through [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), after the abolition of its monarchy.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Nepal_census "2021 Nepal census"), the Hindu population in Nepal is estimated to be around 23,677,744 which accounts for at least 81.19%[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) of the country's population, the highest percentage of [Hindus of any country in the world](/wiki/Hinduism_by_country "Hinduism by country").[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Vikram Samvat](/wiki/Vikram_Samvat "Vikram Samvat"), one of the two official calendars used in Nepal, is a [solar calendar](/wiki/Solar_calendar "Solar calendar") essentially the same to that widespread in [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") as a religious calendar, and is based on [Solar unit](/wiki/Solar_unit "Solar unit") of time.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") is the [largest religion](/wiki/Religion_in_Nepal "Religion in Nepal") in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 2006, the country declared itself a [secular country](/wiki/Secular_country "Secular country") through [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy"), after the abolition of its monarchy.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Nepal_census "2021 Nepal census"), the Hindu population in Nepal is estimated to be around 23,677,744 which accounts for at least 81.19%[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) of the country's population, the highest percentage of [Hindus of any country in the world](/wiki/Hinduism_by_country "Hinduism by country").[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Vikram Samvat](/wiki/Vikram_Samvat "Vikram Samvat"), one of the two official calendars used in Nepal, is a [solar calendar](/wiki/Solar_calendar "Solar calendar") essentially the same to that widespread in [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") as a religious calendar, and is based on [Solar unit](/wiki/Solar_unit "Solar unit") of time.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Among the ethnic groups are the [Bahun](/wiki/Bahun "Bahun"), [Thakuri](/wiki/Thakuri "Thakuri"), [Tharu](/wiki/Tharu_people "Tharu people"), [Chhetri](/wiki/Chhetri "Chhetri"), [Magars](/wiki/Magars "Magars"), [Hill Dalits](/wiki/Caste_system_in_Nepal "Caste system in Nepal"), [Madheshi](/wiki/Madheshi_people "Madheshi people"), [Newari](/wiki/Newar_people "Newar people") people.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5) Meanwhile, among the major ethnic groups [Sherpa](/wiki/Sherpa_people "Sherpa people"), [Rai](/wiki/Rai_people "Rai people"), [Limbu](/wiki/Limbu_people "Limbu people"), [Gurung](/wiki/Gurung "Gurung") and [Tamang](/wiki/Tamang_people "Tamang people") have lowest percentage of followers of Hinduism within the group.[[5]](#cite_note-:8-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Historians and local traditions say that a [Hindu sage](/wiki/Hindu_saints "Hindu saints") named "Ne" established himself in the [valley of Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu_Valley "Kathmandu Valley") during prehistoric times, and that the word "Nepal" means "the place protected ("pala" in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit")) by the sage Ne". He performed religious ceremonies at Teku, the confluence of the [Bagmati](/wiki/Bagmati_River "Bagmati River") and [Bishnumati](/wiki/Bisnumati_River "Bisnumati River") rivers. According to legends, he selected a pious cowherd to be the first of the many kings of the [Gopala dynasty](/wiki/Gopal_Bansa "Gopal Bansa").[[9]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-9) These rulers are said to have ruled Nepal for over 500 years. He selected Bhuktaman to be the first king in the line of the Gopala (*Cowherd*) dynasty.[[10]](#cite_note-The_Ancient_Period-10) The Gopala dynasty ruled for 621 years. Yakshya Gupta was the last king of this dynasty.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

According to *[Skanda Purana](/wiki/Skanda_Purana "Skanda Purana")*, a rishi called "Ne" or "Nemuni" used to live in [the Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the *Pashupati Purana*, he is mentioned as a saint and a protector.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He is said to have practiced [penance](/wiki/Tapasya "Tapasya") at the [Bagmati](/wiki/Bagmati_River "Bagmati River") and [Kesavati](/wiki/Kesava_Pillai_of_Kandamath "Kesava Pillai of Kandamath") rivers and to have taught his doctrines there too.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma201967–75Grieve2006783–791Levy1990190–191-14)

In the mid-18th century, [Prithvi Narayan Shah](/wiki/Prithvi_Narayan_Shah "Prithvi Narayan Shah"), a [Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") king, set out to put together what would become present-day Nepal. He embarked on his mission by securing the neutrality of the bordering mountain kingdoms. After several bloody battles and sieges, notably the [Battle of Kirtipur](/wiki/Battle_of_Kirtipur "Battle of Kirtipur"), he managed to conquer the Kathmandu Valley in 1769.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Timeline of Hinduism in Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Timeline of Hinduism in Nepal")]

#### Medieval Era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Medieval Era")]

The Gurkha control reached its height when the North Indian territories of the [Kumaon](/wiki/Kumaon_Kingdom "Kumaon Kingdom") and [Garhwal Kingdoms](/wiki/Garhwal_Kingdom "Garhwal Kingdom") in the west to [Sikkim](/wiki/Sikkim "Sikkim") in the east came under Nepalese control. A dispute with [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") over the control of mountain passes and inner Tingri valleys of Tibet forced the [Qing Emperor](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") of China to start the [Sino-Nepali War](/wiki/Sino-Nepalese_War "Sino-Nepalese War"), compelling the Nepali to retreat to their own borders in the north.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELandon192868–69-16) The rivalry between the Kingdom of Nepal and the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") over the control of states bordering Nepal eventually led to the [Anglo-Nepali War](/wiki/Anglo-Nepalese_War "Anglo-Nepalese War") (1815–1816). At first, the British underestimated the Nepali and were soundly defeated until committing more military resources than they had anticipated needing. Thus began the reputation of [Gurkhas](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") as fierce and ruthless soldiers. The war ended in the [Sugauli Treaty](/wiki/Sugauli_Treaty "Sugauli Treaty"), under which Nepal ceded recently captured lands.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBooks201989–90-17)

Factionalism inside the royal family led to a period of instability. In 1846, a plot was discovered revealing that the reigning queen had planned to overthrow [Bir Narsingh Kunwar](/wiki/Jung_Bahadur_Rana "Jung Bahadur Rana"), a fast-rising military leader. This led to the [Kot massacre](/wiki/Kot_massacre "Kot massacre"); armed clashes between military personnel and administrators loyal to the queen led to the execution of several hundred princes and chieftains around the country. Bir Narsingh Kunwar emerged victorious and founded the [Rana dynasty](/wiki/Rana_dynasty "Rana dynasty"), and came to be known as [Jung Bahadur Rana](/wiki/Jung_Bahadur_Rana "Jung Bahadur Rana"). The king was made a titular figure, and the post of Prime Minister was made powerful and hereditary. The Ranas were staunchly pro-British and assisted them during the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") (and later in both World Wars). In 1860, some parts of the western Terai region were gifted to Nepal by the British as a friendly gesture because of their military support to sustain British control in India during the rebellion (known as [Naya Muluk](/wiki/Naya_Muluk "Naya Muluk") or "new country"). In 1923, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and Nepal formally signed [an agreement of friendship](/wiki/Nepal%E2%80%93Britain_Treaty_of_1923 "Nepal–Britain Treaty of 1923") that superseded the Sugauli Treaty of 1816.[[18]](#cite_note-auto-18)

The Hindu practice of [Sati](/wiki/Sati_(practice) "Sati (practice)"), in which a widow sacrifices herself in the funeral pyre of her husband, was banned in 1919, and [slavery](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Asia "History of slavery in Asia") was officially abolished in 1924.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutt200422-19) Rana rule was marked by tyranny, [debauchery](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/debauchery "wiktionary:debauchery"), economic exploitation, and religious persecution.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Early-modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early-modern era")]

In the time of early-modern era in Nepal, [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") was at the peak of its prominence. The [Shah rulers](/wiki/Shah_dynasty "Shah dynasty") focused on the Hinduization of Nepal; even then there were good relations of [Nepalis Hindus](/wiki/Nepalis "Nepalis") with the [Nepali Muslims](/wiki/Nepali_Muslim "Nepali Muslim").[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Nepal rulers passed laws making [conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion") from [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") to [Islam and Christianity](/wiki/Islam_and_Christianity "Islam and Christianity") illegal and enacting them as criminal offenses.[[23]](#cite_note-23) These laws were enforced even after the [revolution of 1951](/wiki/Revolution_of_1951 "Revolution of 1951"), and were reaffirmed in the [legal code](/wiki/General_Code_of_Nepal "General Code of Nepal") of 1963, which prohibited the preaching of Christianity or Islam and stipulated three years in jail for those who attempted to convert people, and six years for those who succeeded in converting others. For those who "attempt" to be converted, there was a fine of a hundred rupees, and for those who actually converted (that is, were [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism")), there would be imprisonment of one year. The code stated that "when somebody becomes converted, the conversion is nullified, and he remains in the [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") *[dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma")* [religion]".[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

From the early 1960s, the state began to actively prosecute Christians in places where the baptism of [Nepali citizens](/wiki/Nepalis "Nepalis") had occurred; this active governmental persecution continued up to 1990. Following baptisms in [Nepalgunj](/wiki/Nepalgunj "Nepalgunj") and [Tansen](/wiki/Tansen,_Nepal "Tansen, Nepal") between 1958 and 1960, [pastors](/wiki/Pastors "Pastors") David Mukhia and Prem Pradhan, along with six baptized believers, were prosecuted by the authorities for [proselytism](/wiki/Proselytism "Proselytism") and [conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellnerPfaff-CzarneckaWhelpton2012120–126-26) The pastors were sentenced to six years imprisonment; the male converts were sentenced to one year imprisonment and the female converts to six months. Prosecutions such as this continued for the whole of the [Panchayat](/wiki/Panchayat_(Nepal) "Panchayat (Nepal)") period: when an [amnesty](/wiki/Amnesty "Amnesty") was proclaimed in 1990, there were 30 individuals in Nepal imprisoned for crimes of proselytism or conversion, and 200 others who were subject to legal action for the same offenses.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

#### Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Modern era")]

After the [overthrow](/wiki/Revolution_of_1951 "Revolution of 1951") of the [Rana regime](/wiki/Rana_dynasty "Rana dynasty") in 1951, [King Tribhuvan](/wiki/Tribhuvan_of_Nepal "Tribhuvan of Nepal") opened Nepal's borders and appealed to the outside world to assist in Nepal's development.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJain2016956-28) Then, he granted the [freedom of religion to Nepalese](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Nepal "Freedom of religion in Nepal"), especially to [Nepali Muslims](/wiki/Nepali_Muslims "Nepali Muslims"), and the first church was established in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrieve200689-29) Hinduism is the state religion of Nepal.

## Hinduization by rulers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hinduization by rulers")]

According to various historical sources, even though the presence of *varna* and caste had been known as an element in the social structure of the Kathmandu Valley since the [Licchavi](/wiki/Licchavi_(kingdom) "Licchavi (kingdom)") period (c., 3rd century CE), the majority of the residents of the Nepal Valley were for the first time codified into a written code only in the 14th century in the *[Manava Nyaya Shastra](/wiki/Manav_Nyaya_Shastra "Manav Nyaya Shastra")* by the king [Jayasthithi Malla](/wiki/Jayasthitimalla "Jayasthitimalla") (1354–1395 A.D.).[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy199076-30) Jayasthithi Malla, with the aid of five [Kānyakubja](/wiki/Kanyakubja_Brahmins "Kanyakubja Brahmins") and [Maithil](/wiki/Maithil "Maithil") Brahmins whom he invited from the bordering Indian plains, divided the population of the valley into four major classes (*varna*)—[Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin"), [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya"), [Vaishya](/wiki/Vaishya "Vaishya"), [Shudra](/wiki/Shudra "Shudra")—derived from the ancient Hindu text [Manusmriti](/wiki/Manu_Smriti "Manu Smriti") and based on individual's occupational roles.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher1978487-31) The four classes *varna* encompassed a total of 64 castes *jat* within it, with the Shudras being further divided into 36 sub-castes.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Prithvi_Narayan_Shah.jpg/250px-Prithvi_Narayan_Shah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prithvi_Narayan_Shah.jpg)

[Prithvi Narayan Shah](/wiki/Prithvi_Narayan_Shah "Prithvi Narayan Shah"), founder of the [Kingdom of Nepal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Nepal "Kingdom of Nepal")

After the Gurkhali conquest of [Kathmandu Valley](/wiki/Kathmandu_Valley "Kathmandu Valley"), King [Prithvi Narayan Shah](/wiki/Prithvi_Narayan_Shah "Prithvi Narayan Shah") expelled the Christian Capuchin missionaries from [Patan](/wiki/Patan,_Nepal "Patan, Nepal") and revisioned Nepal as *[Asal Hindustan](/wiki/Hindustan#Kingdom_of_Nepal_usage "Hindustan")* (*Real Land of [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus")*).[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirkenholtz201867–71Sharma2019145–147-33) The [Tagadharis](/wiki/Tagadhari "Tagadhari"), thread wearing Hindus of higher categorization, enjoyed the privileged status in the Nepalese capital and more access to the central power after the Gurkhali King Prithvi Narayan's conquest of Kathmandu Valley.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDharam_Vir198865-34)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorgström198011-35) Since then Hinduization became the significant policy of the [Kingdom of Nepal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Nepal "Kingdom of Nepal").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The Nepali [civil code](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") Muluki Ain was commissioned by [Jung Bahadur Rana](/wiki/Jung_Bahadur_Rana "Jung Bahadur Rana") after his European tour and enacted in 1854. It was rooted in traditional [Hindu Law](/wiki/Hindu_Law "Hindu Law") and codified social practices for several centuries in Nepal.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The law also comprised *[Prāyaścitta](/wiki/Pr%C4%81ya%C5%9Bcitta "Prāyaścitta")* (avoidance and removal of sin) and *[Ācāra](/wiki/%C4%80c%C4%81ra "Ācāra")* (the customary law of different castes and communities). It was an attempt to include the entire Hindu as well as non-Hindu population of Nepal of that time into a single hierarchic civic code from the perspective of the [Khas](/wiki/Khas "Khas") rulers.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The Hinduization of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") was mainly predominant in [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu") and the adjoining regions near the Valley.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellnerPfaff-CzarneckaWhelpton2012118-40) [Nepali society](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") has been known for its interfaith religious harmony and tolerance, but the Hinduization and [Saffronisation](/wiki/Saffronisation "Saffronisation") of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") by the [Shah dynasty](/wiki/Shah_dynasty "Shah dynasty"), especially by [Prithvi Narayan Shah](/wiki/Prithvi_Narayan_Shah "Prithvi Narayan Shah"), were seen as the persecution of other religious communities. After that time, until the 1940s, propagation of any other faith than Hinduism was prohibited.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma2019–Tree201490–93-41) The Hindu community was given special rights and even more rights than the other religious community, though freedom of religion was present in the Kingdom of Nepal.

Then, there was the era of [Rana dynasty](/wiki/Rana_dynasty "Rana dynasty"), which was composed mainly of [Kshatriya](/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya") Hindus. Though in the regime of Rana dynasty, Nepal did not witness much Hinduization, but there were still strict Hindus law.

## Hindu symbolism of Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Hindu symbolism of Nepal")]

The pennant is an important Hindu flag that flutters atop [Hindu temples](/wiki/Hindu_temples "Hindu temples").[[42]](#cite_note-:1-42)

Popular tradition holds that [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") had organized the [Nepali people](/wiki/People_of_Nepal "People of Nepal") and given them their flag, with the sun and moon as emblems on it.[[43]](#cite_note-:2-43) In a [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") [Purana](/wiki/Purana "Purana"), it is written that it was Shiva who handed the flag to Vishnu, and then Vishnu to Indra, for the purpose for battling demons.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Impact of Hinduism in modern Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Impact of Hinduism in modern Nepal")]

Hindu and Buddhist traditions in Nepal go back more than two [millennia](/wiki/Millennia "Millennia").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarua201519-45) In [Lumbini](/wiki/Lumbini "Lumbini"), Buddha was born, and [Pashupatinath temple](/wiki/Pashupatinath_Temple "Pashupatinath Temple") in Kathmandu is an old and famous [Shiva temple](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") of Hindus. Nepal has several other temples and Buddhist [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries"), as well as places of worship for other religious groups.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Traditionally, Nepalese [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") thoughts are ingrained with the Hindu and Buddhist philosophical ethos and traditions, which include elements of [Kashmir Shaivism](/wiki/Shaivaites "Shaivaites"), Nyingma School of [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism"), works of Karmacharyas of Bhaktapur, and tantric traditions. [Tantric](/wiki/Tantra "Tantra") traditions are deep rooted in Nepal, including the practice of animal sacrifices. Five types of animals, always male, are considered acceptable for sacrifice: [water buffalo](/wiki/Domestic_buffalo "Domestic buffalo"), [goats](/wiki/Goats "Goats"), [sheep](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep"), [chickens](/wiki/Chickens "Chickens"), and [ducks](/wiki/Ducks "Ducks"). Cows are very sacred animals and are considered as mothers and banned from killing.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Nepal has been the home of many ancient sites of the Hinduism and is the hub for the tourism for many Hindu pilgrimages.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Relations with other communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Relations with other communities")]

### [Hinduism and Buddhism](/wiki/Hinduism_and_Buddhism "Hinduism and Buddhism")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hinduism and Buddhism")]

There has traditionally been a great deal of intermingling of Hindu and [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") beliefs.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Many people regarded as Hindus in the 1981 census could also in some senses be called Buddhists. Hindus long have worshipped at Buddhist temples and Buddhists at Hindu temples.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBirkenholtz201898–105Books2019viii-56) The reason for this is that both Hinduism and Buddhism have [common roots](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions"), and over most of their history have not been seen as separate groups, but rather cooperative sects with shared religious traditions.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Because of such dual faith practices (or mutual respect), the differences between Hindus and Buddhists have been very subtle and academic in nature; Hindus and Buddhists have never engaged in any religious conflicts for past millennia.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) There are many temples where both [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") can enter and worship.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### [Hinduism and Islam](/wiki/Hinduism_and_Islam "Hinduism and Islam")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hinduism and Islam")]

Though historically, there have been no major conflict between the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") in the Nepal,[[63]](#cite_note-:4-63) though in the 20th century there have been some controversies between the two communities due to the religious conversions and strict laws against the same.[[64]](#cite_note-64) There have been claims of increases in [Islamophobia](/wiki/Islamophobia "Islamophobia") in Nepal by local Hindus and Buddhists as a result of the rise of [Hindutva](/wiki/Hindutva "Hindutva") in India and the prejudice against [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") by [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) However, this is reported to have had no effect in the community level, reflected by demands of Muslims to convert Nepal into a Hindu State.[[67]](#cite_note-:5-67) This is because they feel their religion is not threatened by the other and that they have shared in a spirit of brotherhood for decades. Both see the Expansion of Christianity as a common problem.[[63]](#cite_note-:4-63)[[68]](#cite_note-:6-68)

As a result, during the protests for Nepal re-declaration as a Hindu state, many Muslims supported the movement for [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") as a हिंदू राष्ट्र (transl. Hindu nation).[[68]](#cite_note-:6-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### [Hinduism and Christianity](/wiki/Hinduism_and_Christianity "Hinduism and Christianity")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hinduism_in_Nepal&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hinduism and Christianity")]

In Nepal, the relations between the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Nepal "Christianity in Nepal") have many often been a subject of controversy.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaharjan20021–2-70) The expansion of Christianity is a controversial subject in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), and Nepali Christians have been subject to sporadic violence and widespread [social exclusion](/wiki/Social_exclusion "Social exclusion") by the local Hindus and Muslims.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) It is frequently claimed in Nepali media and political discourse that missionaries offer the poor material incentives to convert with necessary proof but these proofs are often left with no attention.[[73]](#cite_note-:7-73) There has been number of increase of conflicts between the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") of Nepal, due to the conversion of the poor and uneducated Hindus by the [Christian Pastors](/wiki/Pastor "Pastor") and [Missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries").[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-:7-73) There have often been conflicts between the Hindus with Christians in Nepal, among the land and other cultural disputes.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharma201978-75) The [Catholic Church of Nepal](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Nepal "Catholic Church in Nepal") is one of the fastest growing churches in the world, due to which the population and demographics of the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") of [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") is decreasing leading to the serious tensions between the two communities.[[76]](#cite_note-76) There have been several incident reporting the conflicts between the two communities. The rise of [Hindu nationalism](/wiki/Hindu_nationalism "Hindu nationalism") in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") is seen as a threat on non-[Sanatani](/wiki/Sanatani "Sanatani") religions in Nepal. The persecution(rare) mainly occurs as attacks on tribal people who [converted to Christianity](/wiki/Converted_to_Christianity "Converted to Christianity") by other tribal people, destruction of churches and a ban on [proselytization](/wiki/Proselytism "Proselytism").

As seen from the [2001](/wiki/2001_Nepal_census "2001 Nepal census") and [2011 Census](/wiki/2011_Nepal_census "2011 Nepal census") data, the percentage of Hindus has gone up by 0.72%, from 80.62% to 81.34%. However, the overall trend remains largely negative. All the major racial group except the Sino/Tibetans showed a decline in the percentage of Hindus, which was especially sharp among certain Adivasi groups such as [Tharu](/wiki/Tharu_people "Tharu people"). Among the Sino/Tibetans, the percentage of Hindus went up by 2.37%, from 49.74% to 52.11%.

  Nepali Hindu bride and groom

Currently, Nepal is a secular country, as declared by the Constitution of Nepal 2072 (Part 1, Article 4), where secularism 'means religious, cultural freedom, along with the protection of religion, culture handed down from time immemorial (सनातन)'.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Nepal remained the last [Hindu nation](/wiki/Hindu_nationalism "Hindu nationalism") until 2008, and still Nepal has a Hindu majority population. It has the highest Hindu population in the world, after [India](/wiki/Hinduism_in_India "Hinduism in India").[[86]](#cite_note-86) By percentage, [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") has the highest Hindu population in the world.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) Although many government policies throughout history have disregarded or marginalized minority religions, [Nepalese](/wiki/Nepalis "Nepalis") societies generally enjoy [religious tolerance](/wiki/Religious_tolerance "Religious tolerance") and harmony among all religions, with only isolated incidents of religiously motivated violence.[[67]](#cite_note-:5-67) Nepal's constitution does not give anyone the right to convert any person to another religion. Nepal also passed a more stringent anti-conversion law in 2017.[[89]](#cite_note-:3-89)
